a_image.alt: Swiss Graphic Style style mood board — Design Movement · International Style

sections.a_eyebrow: Grid · Sans-Serif · Professional · Balanced · Clean UI
sections.a_description: Radical clarity through mathematical precision — the Swiss Graphic Style strips design to its purest essentials: sans-serif type, strict grid alignment, generous whitespace, and a single bold accent color against monochrome. Born in the 1950s at Basel and Zurich design schools, it proved that impeccable typography, systematic grids, and disciplined restraint could create designs of timeless power.

Swiss Graphic Style is the foundation of corporate identity worldwide — you'll see its DNA in airline branding, museum signage systems, and multinational corporate identities. It appears on architecture firm sites, design education platforms, and typographic specimen pages — anywhere that treats systematic clarity as the highest design virtue.

a_image_cover.alt: Swiss Graphic Style style use case illustration
Swiss Graphic Style style use case illustration
Swiss Graphic Style style use case illustration

sections.a_description: The Swiss International Typographic Style — radical clarity through grid systems, sans-serif typography, and mathematical precision. Pure white background (#ffffff). Content organized on a strict invisible grid. A bold red (#e53935) accent — the color of the Swiss flag — appears only once: a thick left-border on the heading or a red rule line. Everything else is black (#000000), white, and gray (#888). Zero decoration. Zero border-radius. The design communicates through hierarchy alone: size, weight, and position. Set in Inter — a modern interpretation of the Helvetica/Univers tradition that defined Swiss graphic design, with metrics optimized for screen. Heading at weight 700, 32px, uppercase, 1px letter-spacing, line-height 1.1, black. Eyebrow at weight 400, 11px, uppercase, 4px letter-spacing, color #888. Body at weight 400, 15px, line-height 1.6, color #333. Buttons: primary — black fill, white text, 0 border-radius. Secondary: transparent, 1px black border. All interactions are minimal — hover changes are subtle. Alternatives: Helvetica Neue (the original Swiss choice), Univers (Frutiger's masterpiece), Jost (modern geometric).
